albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: With Veridis Quo, Digital Love, and One More Time all in your all-time top tracks, Discovery is the album that clearly shaped you — time to let it wash over you again.

albums.title: OK Computer
albums.reason: Reckoner and True Love Waits sit deep in your all-time plays, and OK Computer is the Radiohead record that started it all — it deserves a full revisit.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: Sexy Boy and La femme d'argent are cemented in your all-time top tracks, and Moon Safari is the record that put Air on the map — pure late-night bliss from start to finish.

albums.title: Sam's Town
albums.reason: Mr. Brightside, A Dustland Fairytale, and All These Things That I've Done are all deep in your all-time tracks — Sam's Town is the Killers at their most epic and emotionally raw.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is one of your most-played all-time tracks, and Leftism is the record it lives on — a landmark of British electronic music that rewards every replay.

albums.title: Cross
albums.reason: Phantom Pt. II and Genesis are both in your all-time top 50 tracks, and Cross is the defining Justice album — loud, distorted, and absolutely timeless.

albums.title: Overgrown
albums.reason: James Blake is your 17th most-played all-time artist and Postpone is your #1 all-time track — Overgrown is where his sound fully bloomed, with Retrograde and Life Round Here at its peak.

albums.title: The North Borders
albums.reason: Bonobo sits in your all-time and recent 12-month listening, and The North Borders is his most cohesive statement — cinematic, warm, and built for deep listening.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: Rusty Nails appears in both your all-time top tracks and recent listening, and Moderat II is the record it lives on — darker and more emotional than their debut.

albums.title: There Is Love in You
albums.reason: Four Tet is active in your last 12 months and Angel Echoes from this album is deep in your track history — There Is Love in You remains one of the most quietly euphoric electronic records of its era.

albums.title: Portraits
albums.reason: Maribou State sits in your all-time top 50 and Beginner's Luck is one of your most-played tracks — Portraits is their richest album, full of the layered, textural sound you clearly love.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: Trentemøller is a long-standing favourite at 560 all-time plays, and The Last Resort is where his signature dark, cinematic sound was fully formed — a perfect late-night revisit.

albums.title: Meet the Skimmers
albums.reason: Your deep love of Plaid and Boards of Canada points straight to Isan — this Warp-adjacent duo make warm, intricate electronica full of the same bittersweet melody and texture you clearly gravitate toward.

albums.title: Faking the Books
albums.reason: Fans of Air, Moby, and the quieter side of Daft Punk often find Lali Puna irresistible — their blend of soft vocals, electronic production, and dreamy melancholy is right in your lane.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Wait — you already know Röyksopp deeply. Let me redirect: Röyksopp's closest sonic neighbour you may have missed is Erlend Øye's solo work, but for pure discovery try Lemon Jelly — Lost Horizons (2002) is a warm, woozy British electronic gem that sits right between your love of Air and The Avalanches.

albums.title: Lost Horizons
albums.reason: Your huge love of The Avalanches and Air points directly here — Lemon Jelly's Lost Horizons is a playful, sample-rich, deeply warm British electronic record that sounds like a long-lost cousin to Since I Left You.

albums.title: Sex Dreams and Denim Jeans
albums.reason: Your massive Justice and Daft Punk plays signal a clear love of the Ed Banger sound — Uffie was the label's secret weapon and this album is a flirty, fizzy time capsule of that exact Paris electro moment.

albums.title: Denies the Day's Demise
albums.reason: Your love of Aphex Twin, Plaid, and The Avalanches suggests you'd respond to Daedelus — a Los Angeles producer who builds impossibly ornate, sample-layered music with real emotional weight.

albums.title: Eskmo
albums.reason: Your deep affection for Orbital, Aphex Twin, and Boards of Canada places you squarely in the zone for Eskmo's self-titled debut — a stunning mix of glitchy beats, orchestral swells, and moments of pure crystalline calm.

albums.title: Simido
albums.reason: Your recent deep dive into Charles Bradley and Syl Johnson shows a real hunger for raw, soulful music with grit — Moonlight Benjamin brings Haitian voodoo tradition and blues-rock together in a way that's unlike anything else.

albums.title: In the Moment
albums.reason: Your recent listening to Portico Quartet, Oscar Peterson Trio, and Bill Evans points to a growing jazz appetite — McCraven's live-chopped, beat-driven approach to jazz is the perfect bridge between that world and your electronic sensibility.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: Your recent heavy rotation of Nils Frahm, Ben Lukas Boysen, and Apparat maps perfectly onto Rival Consoles — this album is taut, modular-driven electronica with real emotional depth.

albums.title: Mangy Love
albums.reason: Your recent plays of Khruangbin and Hermanos Gutiérrez reveal a taste for laid-back, slightly psychedelic Americana — Cass McCombs makes twisted, unhurried songwriting that shares that same dusty, hypnotic quality.

albums.title: Into the Silence
albums.reason: Your recent listening to Oscar Peterson, Bill Evans, and Portico Quartet shows you want jazz that breathes and feels — Israeli trumpeter Avishai Cohen's Into the Silence is quietly devastating, with the same introspective quality as your recent piano favourites.

albums.title: Light as a Feather
albums.reason: Your recent love of Khruangbin, St Germain, and Syl Johnson points toward the warm, groove-driven fusion of Brazilian jazz — Azymuth's Light as a Feather is a stone-cold classic of the genre that Khruangbin themselves have cited as an influence.

albums.title: Con Todo El Mundo
albums.reason: You've been playing Cómo Te Quiero and Maria También on repeat this week — Con Todo El Mundo is the album they live on, and it deserves a full run-through right now.

albums.title: Spells
albums.reason: Golden Times 1 is clearly the track of your week based on your most recent plays — the full Spells album surrounds it with the same luminous, searching energy.

albums.title: Portico Quartet
albums.reason: Ruins has been playing on loop in your recent history — the self-titled debut is where that track lives, and the whole album has that same reverberant, almost ceremonial calm.

albums.title: Ambivalence Avenue
albums.reason: You dropped Lovers' Carvings into your recent queue and it fits perfectly with the dreamy, unhurried mood of your week — the full Ambivalence Avenue album is a gem of warm, hazy electronic folk.

albums.title: Swim
albums.reason: Sun just appeared in your recent plays and its aqueous, shimmering production fits the mood of everything else you've been listening to this week — Swim is one of the great headphone records.

albums.title: Nylon
albums.reason: Planet and Body (Max Cooper Remix) just landed in your queue — Parra for Cuva's debut Nylon has that same featherlight, acoustic-electronic warmth that fits your current contemplative headspace perfectly.

songs.title: Veridis Quo
songs.reason: This is your 6th most-played all-time track and one of Daft Punk's most meditative moments — it deserves to be revisited with full attention.

songs.title: Halcyon and On and On
songs.reason: Orbital sits at 777 all-time plays and Don't Stop Me is deep in your top tracks — Halcyon is their most emotionally overwhelming track and a genuine classic of British electronic music.

songs.title: Eple
songs.reason: Röyksopp are one of your all-time top 5 artists at over 1000 plays — Eple is the track that introduced millions to them and it still sounds completely fresh.

songs.title: Setting Sun
songs.reason: The Chemical Brothers have 556 all-time plays and your track history shows the big anthemic tracks — Setting Sun is their most psychedelic, Noel Gallagher-featuring moment and a genuine era-definer.

songs.title: Safe from Harm
songs.reason: Massive Attack sit at 534 all-time plays and Unfinished Sympathy is already in your history — Safe from Harm is the Blue Lines track that hits just as hard and deserves equal attention.

songs.title: Just a Day
songs.reason: Feeder are your 14th most-played all-time artist at 768 plays and Buck Rogers is in your top 50 tracks — Just a Day is the other Feeder anthem from the same era that absolutely slaps.

songs.title: Cirrus
songs.reason: Bonobo appears in both your all-time and 12-month data, and Cirrus from The North Borders is one of the most euphoric things he's ever made — propulsive, warm, and utterly satisfying.

songs.title: Retrograde
songs.reason: Postpone is your all-time #1 track and CMYK is also in your top 20 — Retrograde from Overgrown is Blake at his most cinematic and deserves to sit alongside them.

songs.title: Angel Echoes
songs.reason: Four Tet is active in your recent 12-month listening and Angel Echoes from There Is Love in You is one of his most achingly beautiful tracks — hypnotic and full of light.

songs.title: Bad Kingdom
songs.reason: Rusty Nails appears in both your all-time and recent track data — Bad Kingdom from Moderat II is equally essential and arguably even more emotionally intense.

songs.title: Tongue
songs.reason: Beginner's Luck is one of your most-played all-time tracks and Maribou State sit in your top 50 — Tongue from Portraits is their most hypnotic, deep-house influenced moment.

songs.title: Take Me Into Your Skin
songs.reason: Trentemøller have 560 all-time plays and your track history already shows a deep connection — Take Me Into Your Skin from The Last Resort is one of his most atmospheric and tension-filled tracks.

songs.title: Space Walk
songs.reason: Your love of The Avalanches and Air is the gateway here — Lemon Jelly's Space Walk is a warm, funny, deeply pleasurable piece of British electronic music that shares that same love of found sound and melody.

songs.title: Lucky Cat
songs.reason: Your deep connection to Plaid and Boards of Canada is the thread — Isan make the same kind of warm, melody-led electronic music and Lucky Cat is one of their most quietly devastating tracks.

songs.title: Go Hawaii
songs.reason: Your love of Boards of Canada and the softer side of Aphex Twin points here — Casino Versus Japan made one of the most beautiful, woozy ambient electronic records of the early 2000s.

songs.title: The Daily Routine
songs.reason: Your all-time love of Moby, Modeselektor, and the more textural side of electronic music suggests Blockhead is a gap worth filling — his instrumental hip-hop is dense, dark, and endlessly replayable.

songs.title: Corymb
songs.reason: Your taste for Plaid, Aphex Twin, and The Avalanches creates a perfect entry point for Boom Bip — Corymb is a lush, sample-heavy, emotionally rich piece of electronic music that sounds like it was made just for you.

songs.title: Awol
songs.reason: Your love of Air, The Shins, and the more delicate end of electronic music points toward Manual — Jonas Munk's project makes quietly gorgeous guitar-ambient music that floats between those worlds effortlessly.

songs.title: Jazz Carnival
songs.reason: Your recent Khruangbin obsession and new interest in St Germain and Oscar Peterson leads naturally here — Jazz Carnival is the track that defines Azymuth's irresistible blend of samba rhythm and jazz improvisation.

songs.title: Temptation Rag
songs.reason: Your recent Bill Evans and Oscar Peterson plays show a deepening jazz interest — Avishai Cohen's trumpet playing on Into the Silence has the same searching, introspective quality that drew you to those records.

songs.title: Anemone
songs.reason: Your recent listening to Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, and A Winged Victory for the Sullen shows a hunger for contemplative, slowly unfolding sound — Danish musician Sofie Birch makes exactly that kind of patient, luminous ambient music.

songs.title: What's a Girl to Do
songs.reason: Your recent DJ Koze and Super Flu plays, combined with your deep love of French house, points toward Fatima Yamaha — this cult Dutch classic is melancholic, danceable, and utterly hypnotic.

songs.title: LesAlpx
songs.reason: Your recent Portico Quartet and Four Tet plays, combined with growing jazz interest, make Floating Points a natural next step — LesAlpx from Elaenia is one of the most euphoric things made at the jazz-electronic intersection.

songs.title: Voices of Bishopsgate
songs.reason: Your recent Portico Quartet, Nils Frahm, and growing jazz appetite points toward Tom Skinner — the Sons of Kemet drummer's solo debut is a meditative, percussion-led spiritual jazz record full of warmth and intention.

songs.title: Cómo Te Quiero
songs.reason: This track literally just played in your queue — it's clearly resonating right now and worth giving a conscious, focused listen rather than just letting it drift past.

songs.title: Golden Times 1
songs.reason: You've played this multiple times in the last seven days — it's clearly the sound you need right now, and it rewards headphone listening with the volume up.

songs.title: Ruins
songs.reason: Ruins is appearing repeatedly in your most recent tracks — it has that rare quality of feeling both ancient and completely present, which seems to be exactly where you are this week.

songs.title: Kaili
songs.reason: You just dropped Sun from Swim into your queue — Kaili from the same album is its dreamy, longer companion and the natural next track to follow where you are right now.

songs.title: Haikuesque (When She Laughs)
songs.reason: Lovers' Carvings just appeared in your recent plays — Haikuesque from the same Ambivalence Avenue album is equally gorgeous, a little more melancholic, and the perfect companion for this mood.

songs.title: Murphy's Law
songs.reason: This track literally just opened your recent plays queue — Róisín Murphy is clearly nudging her way into your current listening and this is a great moment to lean into her fully.
